narration: Explain the precondition that the array must be sorted for binary search to work.
Explain how binary search cuts the search space in half on every comparison.
Explain how to update the low value if the search value is greater than the midpoint.
Explain how to update the high value if the search value is less than the midpoint.
Walk through the binary search algorithm on a small sorted array of eight elements.
Explain the time complexity of binary search as log base 2 n.
Explain the performance benefit of doubling the search space only adds one comparison.
Copy the main function body from a linear search program into the binary search program.
Define the binary search function with an int return type and parameters for array, size, and search value.
Initialize the low variable to zero and the high variable to size minus one.
Calculate the midpoint by adding low and high and dividing by two using integer division.
Compare the search value with the value at the midpoint index.
If the search value is equal to the midpoint value, return the midpoint index.
If the search value is greater than the midpoint value, update low to mid plus one.
If the search value is less than the midpoint value, update high to mid minus one.
Wrap the comparison and update logic in a while loop that continues while low is less than or equal to high.
Return negative one if the search value is not found in the array.
Build and run the binary search program to test the implementation.
Test the binary search function with a value that exists in the array.
